title: Malted bloomer
subtitle: A large, scored oval loaf enriched with malt extract
excerpt: One large oval loaf made with malt extract and strong white flour, scored across the top to control the bloom. The malt gives the crumb a slight chew and a depth of flavour that plain white bread does not have. A good everyday loaf.
sourceNotes: The British bloomer loaf is documented in pre-1928 bakery trade manuals; the malt-enriched version appears in early-twentieth-century British household bread-baking guides and in the trade press of the period. Malt extract as a bread enrichment is described in Florence Jack, Cookery for Every Household (1914, public domain). Cross-referenced with the British baker's textbooks of the period available through the British Library digitised collections.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Toast from frozen or thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping, cover the dough on its tray and refrigerate overnight. Bake straight from the fridge, adding 5 minutes to the bake time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 230°C. High heat for the first 15 minutes gives a good crust; turn down to 200°C fan for the final 20 minutes.
baking.decoratingTechnique: diagonal cuts across the top before baking

glossaryTerms.definition: The diagonal slashes across the top of a bloomer loaf. The cuts allow the dough to expand upward and outward along predetermined lines during the oven spring, rather than bursting randomly at the sides. The number of cuts and their angle determine the final shape: shallow cuts give a rounder bloom, deeper cuts a more dramatic ear. The loaf should be slashed quickly and decisively with a sharp blade to avoid deflating the dough.

content.text: A bloomer is a freeform oval loaf, baked directly on a tray without a tin. The name refers to the way it blooms and expands through the diagonal cuts scored across the top before baking: the
controls the shape of the oven spring. Malt extract goes into the dough for colour and flavour. One large loaf, about 500 g when baked.

Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Dissolve the malt extract in the warm water, then add to the flour along with the softened butter. Mix until a dough forms, then knead for 10 minutes by hand or 8 minutes with a dough hook, until the dough is smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky.
Cover the bowl and leave in a warm place for about 90 minutes, until doubled. Line a large baking tray with baking paper.

Turn the dough out and shape into a firm oval by pulling the sides underneath and rolling it along the bench. Place seam-side down on the lined tray. Cover loosely and leave for 45 minutes. Towards the end of the proof, heat the oven to 220°C fan (230°C conventional).

Mist or brush the top of the loaf with water and scatter over the sesame seeds if using. Using a sharp knife or razor blade held at a 30 to 45 degree angle, make 5 or 6 diagonal cuts across the surface, each about 1 cm deep. Bake at 220°C fan for 15 minutes, then reduce to 200°C fan and bake for a further 20 minutes, until the crust is deep brown and the loaf sounds hollow when tapped on the base. Cool completely on a wire rack before cutting.
